symptom: Blower fan runs continuously and won't turn off, even when thermostat is set to Off or the call for heat/cool has ended. Room is getting cold from recirculated unheated air. No burner activity.
diagnosis: Stuck fan limit switch (snap-disc). The bimetal disc has welded closed or the switch body has failed, so the fan circuit is permanently energized. Alternatively: stuck fan relay on the control board.
procedure: Move the thermostat fan setting from Auto to On, then back to Auto. If the fan obeys On but not Auto, it's likely a limit-switch or board issue, not a wiring fault.
Kill power at the furnace disconnect.
Locate the fan limit switch (small round or rectangular snap-disc, usually mounted in the supply plenum with 2 wires).
Disconnect the two limit-switch wires and cap them safely.
Restore power. If the fan stops: limit switch is bad, replace with HH18HA494 or OEM match.
If the fan still runs: the fault is on the control board — the fan relay is welded. Replace the board.
Install the new limit switch in the same orientation (label side out), reconnect wires, and test.
Verify fan cycles correctly on a complete heat call: burners light → 60-90s delay → fan on → burners off → fan runs 90-120s → fan off.
safety_notes: A fan that won't turn off is a symptom, not a safety hazard on its own — but a furnace with welded limit controls CAN overheat if the heat-exchanger limit also fails. Inspect the high-limit switch too.
Never bypass (jumper out) a limit switch as a 'fix' — this removes the safety that prevents heat-exchanger overheating.
Control boards can retain voltage for 30-60s after power-off. Wait before touching board components.
